content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 15, 2006


H. Geno Acevedo
President-Brewmaster
El Toro Brewing Company
General Partner, Acevedo Properties 1, LLC
Planning Commissioner, City of Morgan Hill
17605 Monterey Road
Morgan Hill, California 95037

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-06-120

Dear Mr. Acevedo:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that the Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing advice; this advice is based solely on the facts that you provide. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

     1. Can you, a Morgan Hill Planning Commissioner, participate in discussions or votes regarding whether the planning commission should recommend that the city extend the term of the redevelopment agency's project timeline?

     2. Can you, a Morgan Hill Planning Commissioner, participate in discussions or votes regarding what the planning commission should recommend to the city regarding the purchase of street furniture for the project area?




_________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-06-120
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

1. You are disqualified from participating in discussions or votes regarding
whether the planning commission should recommend that the city extend the term of the
redevelopment agency's project timeline.

2. You are disqualified from participating in discussions or votes regarding what
the planning commission should recommend to the city regarding the purchase of street
furniture for the project area.

FACTS

You are, and have been for six years, a member of the City of Morgan Hill
Planning Commission ("Planning Commission").

You and your wife are the General Partners in Acevedo Properties 1, LLC
("Acevedo Properties"). You and your wife are also the majority shareholders in a small
closely held S-corporation (called Huntington Beach Brewing Co.), which is doing
business as El Toro Brewing Company Brewpub ("Brewpub").

Approximately one year ago, Acevedo Properties purchased property that was
formerly used by the Morgan Hill Police Department (the "Property")² from the Morgan
Hill Redevelopment Agency ("Redevelopment Agency"). Acevedo Properties is
developing the Property to be used for the location of the Brewpub. ³ As of the time of
your letter, the Brewpub was not yet open, but was anticipated to open soon.

Morgan Hill (the "City") comprise approximately 6,500 acres, of which 2,500
acres located on the northern edge of the City's downtown area are designated as the
Redevelopment Agency's Ojo de Agua project area ("Project Area").

The Property is wholly within, and roughly in the center of, the Redevelopment
Agency's Project Area.

Your letter originally sought advice as to a Planning Commission meeting that
had already taken place on June 13, 2006 – a date that preceded our receipt of your letter.
In our telephone conversation of July 25, 2006, I informed you that we could not provide
advice regarding past conduct. (See Reg. 18329(b)(8)(A) & subd. (c)(4)(A).) You
responded that you did not participate in the Planning Commission's meeting on that date
and, therefore, that you were no longer seeking advice regarding that meeting.

² You have confirmed that your interest in the Property is worth $2,000 or more.

³ You have confirmed that your interest in the Brewpub is worth $2,000 or more.

File No. A-06-120
Page No. 3

However, in your letter, you expressed a desire to obtain advice "as to the extent
of [your] participation, the extent of [your] conflict of interest and what disclosures are
necessary," but you did not specifically describe any future governmental decisions about
which you might be requesting advice. In our July 25, 2006 conversation (and in a
subsequent August conversation), you clarified that you were specifically interested in
knowing whether you could participate in discussions or votes regarding: (1) whether the
Planning Commission should recommend that the City extend the term of the
Redevelopment Agency's Project timeline, and (2) what the Planning Commission
should recommend to the City regarding the purchase of street furniture for the Project
Area.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence governmental decisions in which
the official has a financial interest, unless an exception applies.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Reg. 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
one or more of his or her financial interests. (Section 87103.)

Steps 1 & 2: As A Planning Commissioner, Are You A Public Official
Making, Participating In Making, Or Influencing A Governmental Decision?

As a planning commissioner, you are a member or officer of a local government
agency and are a public official under the Act. (Section 82048; see reg. 18701(a)
[defining "public official"].)⁴ As a planning commissioner you will be called upon to

⁴ If a public official's office is listed in section 87200 ("87200 filers" include city council
members and members of planning commissions) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision
noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item,
verbally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic
interest, as discussed in regulation 18702.5(b)(1)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or
herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed
sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests,
special rules found in regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.) If you want to
speak as a member of the public, you may speak only on behalf of your personal interests. (Reg.
18702.4(b).) You may not speak in a manner which, through words or actions, would lead a listener
reasonably to believe that you are speaking on behalf of another person or entity, e.g., the Planning
Commission. (Ibid.; see Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-04-168.) Since you are a member of the Planning
Commission, these requirements are applicable to you if you determine that you have a conflict of interest
in a governmental decision.

File No. A-06-120
Page No. 4

make, participate in making, and/or influence governmental decisions regarding the
details of the Project. (See regs. 18702 – 18702.4.) Therefore, you will be making,
participating in making, or otherwise using or attempting to use your official position to
influence a governmental decision. (See regs. 18702.1 – 18702.3, enclosed.)

Step 3: Do You Have A Potentially Disqualifying Economic Interest?

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on any of the
following types of interests:

• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment⁵ of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); reg. 18703.1(a)), or in
which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management (section 87103(d); reg. 18703.1(b));

• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); reg. 18703.2; see section 82033
[defining "Interest in Real Property"]);

• An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87103(c); reg. 18703.3);

• An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(e); reg.
18703.4);

• An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the 'personal financial effects' rule. (Section 87103;
reg. 18703.5.)

In addition, a public official also has an economic interest in a business entity that
is a parent or subsidiary of, or is otherwise related to, a business entity in which the
official has one of the interests defined in section 87103(a) or (d). (Reg. 18703.1(c); see
also reg. 18703.1(d) defining "parent-subsidiary" and "otherwise related business
entity".)

⁵For purposes of section 87103, "indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own
directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater." (Section 87103, last paragraph.)

File No. A-06-120
Page No. 5

A. Business Entities.

Acevedo Properties. You and your wife are the general partners in Acevedo
Properties. You also have a direct investment interest in Acevedo Properties worth
$2,000 or more based on your direct ownership and an indirect investment interest based
on your wife's ownership. Because your direct and indirect economic interest in
Acevedo Properties is equal to or greater than $2,000, and you are a partner in that
business entity, your ownership of Acevedo Properties constitutes an economic interest
under the Act. (Section 87103(a) & (d); regs. 18703.1(a).)

The Brewpub. You and your wife are the majority shareholders in the Brewpub.
You have a direct investment interest based on your shares, and an indirect investment
interest based on your wife's shares, in the Brewpub. Therefore, since your economic
interest in the Brewpub is equal to or greater than $2,000, you have an economic interest
in the Brewpub. (Section 87103(a); reg. 18703.1(a).) Moreover, if you are a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or hold any position of management in the Brewpub,
you would also be deemed to have an economic interest in the Brewpub, even if you did
not meet the test described in the preceding sentence. (See section 87103(d); reg.
18703.1(b).)

B. Real Property.

The Property – Real Property. Because you and your wife are the general
partners in the business entity (Acevedo Properties) that owns the Property, you have a
direct or indirect ownership interest of 10% or greater, and the Property is worth at least
$2,000, the Property constitutes an economic interest under the Act. (Sections 82033 and
87103(b); reg. 18703.2.)

Step 4: Is The Economic Interest Directly Or Indirectly Involved In The
Governmental Decision?

Once an official identifies an economic interest, he or she must determine whether
the economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the specific decisions in
question. (Reg.§ 18700(b)(4) & 18704(a).) Having established the degree of
involvement, the official can then identify the materiality standard appropriate to the
circumstances. (Reg. 18700(b)(5).) There are separate tests applied to business entities
and real property to determine whether an economic interest is directly or indirectly
involved in a specific governmental decision. We first address the test applicable to the
business entity.

File No. A-06-120
Page No. 6


A. Whether The Business Entities Are Directly or Indirectly Involved.

A business entity is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency
when if: (1) initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, or (2) is a named party in, or is the subject of, the decision before the official or the official's agency. (Reg. 18704.1(a)(1) & (2).) A
business entity is "the subject of a proceeding" if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject business entity. (Reg. 18704.1(a)(2).)

As stated above, though you did not specifically describe any future governmental
decisions about which you might be requesting advice in your initial letter, in subsequent
phone conversations you clarified that you were specifically interested in knowing
whether you could participate in discussions or votes regarding: (1) whether the Planning
Commission should recommend that the City extend the term of the Redevelopment
Agency's Project timeline, and (2) what the Planning Commission should recommend to
the City regarding the purchase of street furniture for the Project Area. If a business
entity is not directly involved, as described, it is indirectly involved.

Your description of the decisions at issue do not indicate that either Acevedo
Properties or the Brewpub: (1) initiated the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, or (2) is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. (See reg.
18704.1(a)(1) & (2).) Therefore, Acevedo Properties and the Brewpub are indirectly
involved in the decisions before the Planning Commission.

B. Whether Real Property Is Directly or Indirectly Involved.

Regulation 18704.2, in pertinent part, states that real property in which a public
official has an economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if:

"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or
boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision. For purposes of subdivision (a)(5), real property
is located 'within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of
the real property which is the subject of the governmental decision' if any
part of the real property is within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment project area.

¶[...]¶

File No. A-06-120
Page No. 7

"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to
rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which
the official has an interest, or any part of it is located within the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment area."

Concerning the decision as to whether to recommend extending the term of the
Redevelopment Agency's Project timeline, the Property is directly involved. The
Property constitutes real property in which you have an interest and it is located within
500 feet of (indirect, it is located within) the boundaries of the property (i.e., the Project
Area) that is the subject of the governmental decision. (See reg. 18704.2(a)(1).) In
addition, the Property appears to be directly involved because it is located within the
boundaries of the redevelopment area and the decision at issue, as you describe it,
appears that it would amend the adopted form of the preliminary plan of the Project Area
by extending its timeline. (See reg. 18704.2(a)(5).)

Concerning the decision as to what the Planning Commission should recommend
to the City Council regarding the purchase of street furniture for the Project Area, the Property is
also directly involved. The Property constitutes real property in which you have an
interest and it is located within the boundaries of the property that is the subject of the
governmental decision. (See reg. 18704.2(a)(1).) In addition, the Property may also be
directly involved because it is located within the boundaries of the redevelopment area
and the decision at issue, as you describe it, appears that it might involve amending the
adopted form of the preliminary plan of the Project Area with regard to, e.g., the type and
amount of street furniture the City should purchase for the Project Area. (See reg.
18704.2(a)(5).)

Step 5: Is The Financial Effect Of The Governmental Decision On Your
Economic Interest Material?

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regs.
18700(a), 18705.) Different standards apply to determine whether a financial effect will
be material, depending upon the nature of the economic interest and whether that interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision under consideration. (Reg.
18705.2, subds.(a) and (b).)

A. Whether the Financial Effect on the Real Property Is Material.

The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on non-
leasehold, directly involved real property is presumed to be material. (Reg. 18705.2(a).)
Because you have a non-leasehold economic interest in the Property, and the Property is
directly involved, the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the governmental decisions

File No. A-06-120
Page No. 8

directly involved in the two governmental decisions you describe, the financial effect of
each of those decisions are presumed to be material. This presumption may be rebutted
by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any
financial effect, not even "one penny," on the real property. This is known as the "one
penny rule."

B. Whether the Financial Effect on the Business Entities Is Material.

Whether the financial effect of a governmental decision on an indirectly involved
business entity is material or not depends upon what type of business entity is involved.
(See reg. 18705.1(c)(1) – (4), copy enclosed.) There exist different tests depending upon
whether the business entity in question is listed in the Fortune 500, on the New York
Stock Exchange, NASDAQ, American Stock Exchange. (Ibid.) Generally, if a business
entity does not fit any of the preceding lists it is referred sometimes referred to as a
"small business entity" and regulation 18705.1(c)(4) applies.

Assuming either Acevedo Properties or the Brewpub were determined to be a
small business entity (see Reg. 18705.1(c)(4)), the financial effects of a governmental
decision would not be considered material unless the decision under scrutiny impacted
either business as described below:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more." (Reg. 18705.1(c)(4).)

Because you have not provided us with: (1) facts regarding whether your
businesses constitute small business entities under regulation 18705.1(c), or (2) facts
indicating what the effect of the two specific decisions described in this letter might have
on your businesses, we cannot analyze whether the financial effect of the described
governmental decisions would have a material effect on those businesses or not.

However, because your economic interest in the Property is presumed to be
material, determining whether or not the effect of the described decisions on either of
your two businesses is material becomes less relevant; it only takes one disqualifying
economic interest to preclude a public official from participating in a governmental
decision.

File No. A-06-120
Page No. 9


Step 6: Is The Material Financial Effect Reasonably Foreseeable?

Once materiality is established, one must determine whether the material financial
effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable, or substantially likely to occur.
(Reg. 18706.) A financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. It must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC
Ops. 198; see Reg. 18706(b) listing five factors to consider in determining whether the
material financial effect of a governmental decision will be reasonably foreseeable].) A
material financial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is
substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards applicable to the
particular economic interest under analysis will be met as a result of the governmental
decision. (Reg. 18706(a).)

With regard to the Property, the financial effect of either of the governmental
decisions that you have described, upon your non-leasehold real property interest in the
Property, is presumed to be material. (Reg. 18705.2(a)(1).) In the absence of any facts to
the contrary, it is substantially likely that the material financial effect regarding: (1)
whether the Planning Commission should recommend that the City extend the term of the
Redevelopment Agency's Project timeline, or (2) what the Planning Commission should
recommend to the City regarding the purchase of street furniture for the Project Area, is
reasonably foreseeable.

Therefore, without more facts or an applicable exception, you have a
disqualifying economic interest in your Property that would preclude you from voting on
the two governmental decisions you have described.

Steps 7 & 8: The "Public Generally" And "Legally Required" Exceptions.

If an official has a conflict-of-interest regarding a proposed governmental
decision, he or she may still participate in the described governmental decision making
process if either the "public generally" or "legally required participation" exceptions
apply. (See regs. 18707 & 18708.)

If neither exception applies, the conflict of interest is deemed to be a disqualifying
conflict of interest, and you will not be able to make, participate in making or influencing
the governmental decisions described. Because you have not provided us with the facts
necessary to analyze your situation under these exceptions, the following information is
provided as a general guide.

The "Public Generally" Exception

A public official will not be disqualified from participating in a governmental
decision if the proposed decision affects the public official's economic interests in a

File No. A-06-120
Page No. 10

manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the
"public generally." (Section 87103; reg. 18707(a).) Under the two prong test, to meet
the requirements of this exception, the decision must financially affect a "significant
segment" of property owners or homeowners in "substantially the same manner." (Reg.
18707.1.)

For real property interests, the "significant segment" is defined as 10% of
property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction, or the district the official represents
(Reg. 18707.1(b)(1)(B)(i)) or 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction.
If either of these tests applies, then it must be determined
whether the significant segment is affected in "substantially the same manner." (Reg.
18707.1(b)(2).)

Because we do not have sufficient facts to apply this exception, we are unable to
provide further analysis.

The "Legally Required Participation" Exception

You have not provided any facts suggesting that this exception would apply.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Andreas C. Rockas
Counsel, Legal Division

once an official identifies an economic interest, he or she must determine whether

A. Whether The Business Entities Are Directly or Indirectly Involved.
A business entity is directly involved in a decision before an official,s agency

Step 5: Is The Financial Effect Of The Governmental Decision On your
Economic Interest Material?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interestsls material. (Regs.
18700(a), 18705.) Different standards apply to determine whether a financial effect will
be material, depending upon the nature of the economic interest and whether that interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the govemmental decision under consideration. (Reg.
18705.2, subds.(a) and (b).)
A. whether the Financial Effect on the Real property rs Material.
The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on non-
leasehold, directly involved real property is presumed to"be material. (Reg. I g705.2(a).)
Because you have a non-leasehold economiC inbrest in the property, and Ihe property is
directly involved in the two governmental decisions you describe, the financial effect of

B' Whether the Financial Effect on the Business Entities Is Material.
Whether the financial effect of a govemmental decision on an indirectly involved

The "Public Generally" Exception

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1' Can you, a Morgan Hill Planning Commissioner, participate in discussions or
votes regarding whether the planning commission should recommend that the city extend
the term of the redevelopment agency,s project timeline?
2' Can you, a Morgan Hill Planning Commissioner, participate in discussions or
votes regarding what the planning commission should recommendio the city regarding
the purchase of street furniture for the project area?
I Government code sections 8 1 000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
.!i6-,il

CONCLUSION: 1. You are disqualified from participating in discussions or votes regarding
whether the planning commission should recommend that the city extend the term of the
redevelopment agency' s project timeline.
2. Yort are disqualified from participating in discussions or votes regarding what
the planning commission should recommend to the city regarding the purchase of street
furniture for the project area.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 15, 2006 H. Geno Acevedo President-Brewmaster El Toro Brewing Company General Partner, Acevedo Properties 1, LLC Planning Commissioner, City of Morgan Hill 17605 Monterey Road Morgan Hill, California 95037 RE: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-06-120 Dear Mr. Acevedo: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that the Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing advice; this advice is based solely on the facts that you provide. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS 1. Can you, a Morgan Hill Planning Commissioner, participate in discussions or votes regarding whether the planning commission should recommend that the city extend the term of the redevelopment agency's project timeline? 2. Can you, a Morgan Hill Planning Commissioner, participate in discussions or votes regarding what the planning commission should recommend to the city regarding the purchase of street furniture for the project area? _________________________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-06-120 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION 1. You are disqualified from participating in discussions or votes regarding whether the planning commission should recommend that the city extend the term of the redevelopment agency's project timeline. 2. You are disqualified from participating in discussions or votes regarding what the planning commission should recommend to the city regarding the purchase of street furniture for the project area. FACTS You are, and have been for six years, a member of the City of Morgan Hill Planning Commission ("Planning Commission"). You and your wife are the General Partners in Acevedo Properties 1, LLC ("Acevedo Properties"). You and your wife are also the majority shareholders in a small closely held S-corporation (called Huntington Beach Brewing Co.), which is doing business as El Toro Brewing Company Brewpub ("Brewpub"). Approximately one year ago, Acevedo Properties purchased property that was formerly used by the Morgan Hill Police Department (the "Property")² from the Morgan Hill Redevelopment Agency ("Redevelopment Agency"). Acevedo Properties is developing the Property to be used for the location of the Brewpub. ³ As of the time of your letter, the Brewpub was not yet open, but was anticipated to open soon. Morgan Hill (the "City") comprise approximately 6,500 acres, of which 2,500 acres located on the northern edge of the City's downtown area are designated as the Redevelopment Agency's Ojo de Agua project area ("Project Area"). The Property is wholly within, and roughly in the center of, the Redevelopment Agency's Project Area. Your letter originally sought advice as to a Planning Commission meeting that had already taken place on June 13, 2006 – a date that preceded our receipt of your letter. In our telephone conversation of July 25, 2006, I informed you that we could
